children.name: Momotus
children.description: Medium to large motmots with a distinctive racket-tipped tail, created when the bird deliberately barbs off the vanes of two long central tail feathers, leaving bare shafts with terminal tufts. They are perhaps the most recognisable motmots, with a blue crown and a stout, serrated bill.

children.name: Momotus momota
children.description: A widespread motmot found from Mexico through Central America to the Amazon basin. It has a blue crown bordered with black and purple, green upperparts, and a rufous belly. The racket-tipped tail is wagged side to side in a distinctive pendulum motion, especially when a potential predator is detected.

children.name: Eumomota
children.description: A monotypic genus of striking motmot with a turquoise brow stripe and a deeply forked tail. The Turquoise-browed Motmot is one of the most visually arresting birds of the Neotropics, known for its habit of swinging its racket-tipped tail like a pendulum.

children.name: Eumomota superciliosa
children.description: Found from southern Mexico through Central America to Costa Rica, inhabiting open woodlands and forest edges. It has a beautiful turquoise-blue stripe above the eye, a blue throat, and greenish upperparts. It is the national bird of both El Salvador and Nicaragua, where it is known as the torogoz.

children.name: Baryphthengus
children.description: Large motmots with a predominantly rufous plumage and a green back and wings. They are among the most vocal motmots, with low, far-carrying hooting calls that resonate through the rainforest.

children.name: Baryphthengus martii
children.description: A large, striking motmot of lowland rainforests from Honduras through the Amazon basin. It has a rufous head and underparts, green upperparts, a black face mask, and a blue crown. Its call is a deep, resonant, owl-like hoot that can carry for hundreds of metres through the forest.

children.name: Electron
children.description: Medium to large motmots with a green breast and a distinctive blue stripe below the throat. The genus name refers to their bright, metallic colours resembling amber (electron in Greek).

children.name: Electron platyrhynchum
children.description: Found from Honduras south through Central America to the western Amazon basin. It has a green head and breast with a vivid blue throat, a broad black bill, and a rufous belly. It is distinguished from the similar Keel-billed Motmot by its broader bill and the absence of a central blue crown patch.
